findings.title: `sanitize_filename` can panic on long non-ASCII extensions
findings.description: The `ext.len() >= MAX_FILENAME_BYTES` branch slices `s[..MAX_FILENAME_BYTES]` directly. `MAX_FILENAME_BYTES` is a byte count, not a UTF-8 boundary, so a filename whose extension crosses byte 200 can panic at runtime with `byte index is not a char boundary`. This turns an untrusted attachment filename into a bot crash.
findings.suggestion: Use `truncate_to_bytes(&s, MAX_FILENAME_BYTES)` in this branch as well, or otherwise compute a valid char boundary before slicing.

findings.title: Path validation happens after the file write, leaving a symlink escape window
findings.description: The code writes with `tokio::fs::write(&dest_path, &bytes)` and only then canonicalizes `dest_path` / `download_dir` to verify containment. If `.pidory/downloads/<thread>` or the target filename is replaced with a symlink before the write, the write already lands outside the allowed tree before the check runs. The later `remove_file` can then delete the attacker-chosen target as well. This is a classic TOCTOU issue around filesystem writes.
findings.suggestion: Open the destination atomically without following symlinks, for example with `create_new(true)` plus platform-specific `O_NOFOLLOW`/equivalent, and validate the parent directory before writing. Do not rely on post-write canonicalization as the primary defense.

findings.title: Attachments are fetched before queue admission, bypassing the session backpressure limit
findings.description: Both the mid-turn path and the primary path download attachments before calling `data.sessions.send_message(...)`. If the per-session queue is already full, or the message is recalled before dequeue, the bot still performs network I/O and writes files to disk for work that will never be processed. A user can abuse this to consume bandwidth/disk while the queue-full guard remains ineffective.
findings.suggestion: Reserve queue capacity before downloading, or enqueue lightweight attachment metadata first and let the session worker perform the download only after the message is accepted. At minimum, clean up downloaded files when enqueue fails or the message is recalled.

findings.title: Trusted attachment metadata is injected into an ordinary user message
findings.description: `build_user_message_json` wraps downloaded file paths in a `<system-reminder>` block but still sends it as `role: user`. That removes the trust boundary between bot-generated metadata and user-controlled text: the user can type the same tag in message content, and attachment filenames are also reflected into this block. The model cannot reliably distinguish real bot instructions from spoofed ones, which is a prompt-injection/API-boundary problem.
findings.suggestion: Send attachment metadata through a separate trusted channel or structured field instead of embedding pseudo-system tags inside user content. If inline text is unavoidable, escape/quote filenames and avoid using tag syntax that the model is expected to treat specially.

summary: The attachment-download change is close, but it introduces a UTF-8 panic path, a post-write symlink race, queue backpressure bypass, and a trust-boundary issue in how attachment metadata is injected into prompts.
